title: Why Rainbows Always Appear at a 42 Degree Angle
summary: A primary rainbow appears near 42 degrees from the antisolar point because water droplets bend, reflect, and disperse sunlight most strongly at that viewing angle.

lesson.learn: A rainbow is not painted onto a cloud and does not sit at a fixed distance. It is an angle-dependent optical effect centered on the antisolar point, the spot directly opposite the Sun from your viewpoint. When sunlight enters a raindrop, it slows and bends. Some of that light reflects off the back of the drop, then bends again as it exits. Different wavelengths bend by different amounts, so white sunlight spreads into colors. The key is that many rays leaving a spherical drop crowd near a minimum-deviation angle. For red light in the primary rainbow, that angle is about 42 degrees from the antisolar direction; violet appears slightly inside it, near 40 degrees. Your eye receives light from countless droplets that happen to sit on that cone of directions, so you see an arc. Someone standing beside you sees a different set of droplets. The rainbow’s reliability comes from geometry, not location.
lesson.simpler: Rainbows appear around 42 degrees because raindrops send the strongest red light back toward your eyes at that angle. Violet bends more, so it appears slightly inside the arc.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The 42-degree rainbow angle comes from the geometry of sunlight refracting, internally reflecting, dispersing, and emerging from spherical water droplets.
lesson.deeperDive: A raindrop acts like a tiny refracting sphere, not a flat prism. The primary bow uses one internal reflection; the secondary bow uses two, exits at a larger angle near 50 degrees, and reverses the color order. The primary rainbow is brightest near the angle where small changes in incoming ray position produce only small changes in outgoing direction, concentrating light into a caustic. This is why the rainbow has a sharp outer edge and a brighter region inside. The bow is actually a full cone of light around the antisolar line. From the ground, the horizon usually blocks the lower part, so we see an arc. From an aircraft or mountain, a circular rainbow can appear when droplets surround the line of sight.
lesson.funFact: No two observers see exactly the same rainbow because each uses a different set of droplets.

references.title: NOAA NESDIS: What Causes a Rainbow?

people: René Descartes - calculated rainbow geometry using spherical drops
Isaac Newton - explained the color spectrum of white light
places: Rain shafts - regions of falling droplets that can produce rainbows
Waterfalls - spray-filled places where rainbows can appear with the Sun behind the viewer
years: 1637 - Descartes published a mathematical explanation of rainbow angles
1704 - Newton’s Opticks explained white light and color
concepts: Antisolar point - point opposite the Sun from the observer
Dispersion - separation of light into colors by wavelength
Internal reflection - reflection from inside the water droplet
Minimum deviation - geometry that concentrates rainbow light near a specific angle
